claims.evidence_text: A temperature between 0 and 8 °C is considered an optimum vernalization temperature for winter wheat [20], with some modern wheat genotypes meeting vernalization requirements with temperatures between 10 and 15 °C
claims.claim_summary: Optimum vernalization temperature for winter wheat is 0-8 C; some modern genotypes vernalize at 10-15 C.

claims.evidence_text: In terms of duration, the effective vernalization period is reported to be between 30 and 45 days for most genotypes
claims.claim_summary: Effective vernalization period for most winter wheat genotypes is 30-45 days.

claims.evidence_text: Sowing shallower than 2 cm shortens the time between sowing and emergence by reducing the hypocotyl and subcrown requirements for elongation, resulting in faster emergence
claims.claim_summary: Sowing shallower than 2 cm speeds emergence; shallow (1 cm) sowing reached maturity ~5 days earlier than deep (2.5 cm).

claims.evidence_text: the SP reached the three-leaf stage one day earlier than the DP did (ZGS 13)... shallow planted... maturity five days earlier than a deep planting depth
claims.claim_summary: Shallow planting (1 cm) reached three-leaf stage 1 day earlier and maturity 5 days earlier than deep planting (2.5 cm) in winter wheat.

claims.evidence_text: the transition from vegetative to reproductive stages in winter wheat depends mainly on photoperiod, vernalization temperature, and vernalization length
claims.claim_summary: Wheat transition to reproductive stage depends on photoperiod; wheat has photoperiod-sensitive and photoperiod-insensitive types.

claims.evidence_text: On average, the NTVs and HTVs took 147 and 157 days to harvest, respectively.
claims.claim_summary: Time to harvest (physiological maturity, ZGS 87) for winter wheat under normal vernalization speed-breeding was 147 days on average (157 days high-throughput vernalization).
